LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains no code in its global (top-level) scope or in a `pkgver()` function that would execute dangerous commands during sourcing or source fetching. The global scope consists only of variable and array definitions, a simple `case` assignment for `_CARCH`, and a `source` array that points to standard GitHub URLs. No `eval`, backtick, `$()` (outside safe string expansions), `curl`, `wget`, or other command-execution constructs are present at the top level. The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are defined but will not run during the requested commands (`--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `--printsrcinfo`). Therefore, the two specified `makepkg` commands are safe to execute for this PKGBUILD.
</details>
<summary>No malicious top-level code; source fetching is standard and safe.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard practices for a pre-compiled binary package from the AUR. All sources are fetched from the official GitHub repository of the upstream project (`emilien-jegou/oyui`) via HTTPS, including the binary release tarball and the themes documentation. Both source archives have pinned sha256 checksums, ensuring integrity. The `prepare()` function uses `patchelf` to set the runtime interpreter, a common and expected step for prebuilt binaries to ensure compatibility with the target system. The `package()` function installs only the binary and documentation files into the package directory. There is no obfuscation, no unexpected network requests, no exfiltration of data, and no execution of untrusted code beyond the verified upstream release. This is a legitimate and well-formed AUR package.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR binary PKGBUILD, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `.nvchecker.toml` is a configuration file for the `nvchecker` tool, which automates version tracking for AUR packages. It defines a source block for `oyui-bin` that points to the GitHub repository `emilien-jegou/oyui`, instructing nvchecker to check for the latest release with a tag prefix of `v`. This is a standard AUR maintainer workflow—no dangerous commands, obfuscation, or network requests outside of the legitimate GitHub API. The use of `use_latest_release` (unpinned) is normal for packaging tools and does not constitute a supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>Standard nvchecker config; no malicious behavior.</summary>

Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/oyui-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-tojeywe0... Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-tojeywe0 Analyzing oyui-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-tojeywe0) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg` LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD: SAFE
The PKGBUILD is safe to run with `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The global scope and `pkgver()` function (not defined, so defaulting to the static `pkgver=0.2.0`) do not execute any suspicious or dangerous code. The `source` array entries are standard and point to the project's GitHub repository: one downloads a themes documentation file, and the other fetches a release tarball for x86_64. Both URLs are constructed using trusted variables referencing the declared GitHub author and repo. The `sha256sums` are explicitly provided and match the sources, ensuring integrity. No command substitution, network requests, or file operations occur outside of the declared sources during sourcing. The `case` block for `_CARCH` is benign and only sets an internal variable based on `CARCH`. No data exfiltration, dynamic code evaluation, or external payloads are present.
No malicious code in sourcing or source fetch + Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code in sourcing or source fetch[/] Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent... makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent... makepkg --printsrcinfo successful Reviewing required files... [0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD... [0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, THEMES-0.2.0.md... LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD: SAFE
The PKGBUILD is for oyui-bin, a binary package distributing a pre-built release of a TUI merge tool. The source array fetches the release tarball from the project's official GitHub repository under the declared upstream author and project name, using a version tag (`v0.2.0`) that matches `pkgver`. The binary is processed with `patchelf` to set the correct interpreter path, which is a standard step for relocating binaries in Arch Linux packages. Checksums are provided and match the sources. The source also includes a documentation file (themes.md) from the same trusted upstream. All operations—unpacking, patching the interpreter, and installing files to standard system locations—are consistent with safe AUR packaging practices. There are no network requests at runtime, no use of `eval`, no obfuscated commands, and no execution of downloaded scripts. The package only installs files belonging to the application in appropriate directories. The package provides standard documentation and relies on common tools (`patchelf`) within expected usage. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or tampering with system files outside the package's scope.

It contains thematic previews with embedded image references and notes about visual issues (e.g., contrast, accent colors). The content is static, consisting solely of documentation and metadata about themes—no executable code, scripts, or commands are present. There are no instances of `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, `exec`, or other suspicious constructs. The file does not perform network requests, file manipulations, or system calls. It is structured as a README-style asset index, auto-generated via a development script (`dev theme-screens`). The use of HTML `` tags within Markdown is standard for rendering images in documentation. No security risks are identified. This is a benign documentation file commonly found in source repositories.

The file provided is a `README.md` for the `oyui-bin` package, which serves as documentation for the Oyui merge tool. It contains standard informational content, including features, installation instructions (via Cargo and Nix), configuration examples, and integration with Jujutsu and Neovim. All commands and code snippets are illustrative and intended to guide users in setting up and using the software. There is no executable code in this file, nor any obfuscated content, network exfiltration attempts, or suspicious downloads. The installation methods described—`cargo install oyui` and using a Nix flake—are standard and expected for Rust-based tools in the ecosystem. The configuration examples use Rune scripting and LSP integration, which are part of the application's legitimate functionality and not indicative of malicious behavior. No supply-chain attack vectors are present. The file adheres to normal AUR/development documentation practices and poses no security risk.

The provided file is a `CHANGELOG.md`, which is a standard plain-text document used to record notable changes to a software project. It contains release notes for version 0.2.0 and earlier, detailing feature additions, breaking changes, refactorings, and bug fixes related to theming, configuration, and UI enhancements in what appears to be a terminal-based UI application (possibly written in Rust, given the code snippet syntax). The content consists solely of documentation: version headers, descriptive lists, code blocks showing configuration syntax (in Rust-like format), and links to GitHub commits. There is no executable code, scripts, or any kind of command injection. The file does not perform or suggest any system operations, network requests, file manipulations, or execution of commands. It is intended for human reading and does not pose any security risk by itself. All referenced actions (e.g., `theme::set`, `theme::syntax`) are presented as configuration examples, consistent with application customization. The ability to load themes from a file path (`path:/tmp/...`) is a feature description, not an automated behavior, and falls within normal application functionality. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or supply-chain compromise.
Documentation file with no executable code. [2/3] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml... + Reviewed src/oyui-v0.2.0-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/CHANGELOG.md. Status: SAFE -- Documentation file with no executable code. LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml: SAFE
The provided `.nvchecker.toml` file is a configuration for nvchecker, a version checking tool used to monitor upstream releases. It specifies that the `oyui-bin` package should track the latest release from the GitHub repository `emilien-jegou/oyui` using a version prefix `v`. This is a common and legitimate use of nvchecker in AUR packages to automate version updates. There are no network requests, executable commands, or file operations performed by this configuration file. It does not execute code, download binaries, or modify system files. It simply declares a source for version tracking, which is standard practice for maintaining up-to-date AUR packages. The configuration only refers to the project's official GitHub repository, which matches the expected upstream source. There are no signs of obfuscation, suspicious redirects, or external command execution. The file serves a metadata purpose and poses no security risk.
